summary: How to document inspection findings, agreed scope, surface condition, recommendations and job completion into a single, client-ready report a school or childcare centre can file, audit and rely on. A good report is the evidence layer that turns a finished job into a renewed contract.

steps.title: Open with the job identifiers
steps.detail: Start every report with the details that let a client (or an auditor) tie it to the right job: legal business name and ABN of the contractor, site name and address, the client and site contact, the contract or purchase-order reference, and the date(s) of attendance. State plainly which of the four service areas the visit covered — carpet, tile and grout, vinyl strip and seal, or pressure washing — because a strip-and-seal report carries very different risk and documentation from a routine carpet clean.

steps.title: Record the pre-inspection findings
steps.detail: Write up what you found before you started, because pre-inspection is part of the job, not an optional extra. For carpet, note the fibre and construction, the soil load and any pre-existing damage, delamination, prior repairs, wear patterns or permanent staining. For tile and grout, note the tile type, grout condition and any target slip class the site is aiming for. For vinyl, note the existing finish and substrate condition. Recording pre-existing damage up front protects the client and protects you commercially — a problem that was already there cannot later be blamed on your visit.

steps.title: Capture dated before-condition evidence
steps.detail: Photograph the areas to be serviced before any work begins, with enough context (a doorway, a fixed feature, a room label) that each image is clearly identifiable later. Keep the images dated and tied to the room or zone. Photographic before-and-after evidence is what lets a client see the result objectively and settles any question about the starting condition of a floor in an occupied facility.

steps.title: State the agreed scope of works per surface
steps.detail: Set out clearly what was agreed for each surface and the method chosen, with the reasoning. For carpet, name the method — hot water extraction for deeper soil removal, or encapsulation as a low-moisture option that returns a room to service faster in an occupied site. For tile and grout, describe the pre-wet, apply, dwell, agitate, rinse and recover approach. For vinyl, set out the strip, rinse and neutralise, dry, then multiple thin sealer and finish coats sequence at a high level. Make clear that dilution ratios, coat counts and any drying or re-entry steps are governed by each product's Safety Data Sheet (SDS) and Technical Data Sheet (TDS), not by a fixed figure in the report.

steps.title: List the products used and reference the SDS register
steps.detail: Record every chemical brought on site by product name and supplier code (for example a stripper, prespray, spotter, alkaline hard-floor cleaner or sealer), and confirm a current SDS is held for each in an accessible hazardous chemicals register. An SDS is treated as current when it is no more than five years old, and each sheet states its last-reviewed date. Where a product lacked a current SDS, record that you obtained the current SDS from the supplier before use — never guess or invent an SDS code.

steps.title: Document the WHS controls you applied
steps.detail: Record the work health and safety controls used on the job: the SWMS or JSEA prepared for higher-risk tasks (such as vinyl strip and seal or pressure washing), the PPE worn as directed by each product SDS, the ventilation provided, spill-control measures, and how wet or freshly treated floors were isolated and signed. Note that all portable electrical equipment used on site was within its AS/NZS 3760 test-and-tag interval and run on RCD (safety-switch) protection. Reference your state WHS regulator where relevant (SafeWork NSW, WorkSafe Victoria or Workplace Health and Safety Queensland), noting Victoria works under its own OHS Act and Regulations.

steps.title: Record the child-safety measures
steps.detail: In a school or childcare setting the report must show how children were protected. Record that the workers on site held the correct state or territory Working With Children check plus any site-specific child-safe induction, that lower-tox products suited to occupied spaces were selected, and that wet, freshly stripped or freshly sealed areas were physically isolated and signed and kept away from children. Note how the work was scheduled around occupancy or into a term break or holiday window. Frame this as supporting the centre's obligations under National Quality Standard Quality Area 2 (children's health and safety) — you support that intent, you do not certify the centre against it.

steps.title: Record run-off control for any pressure washing
steps.detail: Where the visit included pressure washing, document how wash water was controlled, because stormwater generally flows untreated to waterways and contaminated run-off must be kept out of it. Record the containment used (booms, absorbent socks, temporary bunding, covers over nearby stormwater pits), the recovery method (wet vacuum or vacuum-recovery), and the trade-waste agreement or written approval from the water authority that allowed discharge to sewer. Note that Victorian sites fall under EPA Victoria, which treats contaminated wash water as industrial waste.

steps.title: Record slip-resistance verification where a floor was sealed
steps.detail: Cleaning and sealing can change how slip-resistant a floor is, so where you stripped and sealed a hard floor, record the slip-resistance verification. AS 4663 is the correct basis to measure an existing, in-service surface against a target slip class — as distinct from AS 4586, which classifies new surfaces before installation. Record the target slip class agreed with the site (with reference to SA HB 198 and the National Construction Code, not a rating value carried from memory) and either the AS 4663 result or a note that on-site measurement has been arranged. Be explicit that AS 4663 measures slip resistance only; it does not set cure or re-entry times.

steps.title: Capture after-condition evidence and completion status
steps.detail: Photograph the finished areas from comparable positions to the before images, and record the completion status of each surface: complete, part-complete, or deferred (for example, a periodic strip and seal moved to the next holiday window). Where a floor is still curing at the time of writing, say so, and cross-reference the return-to-service and post-care handover rather than implying the area is immediately ready for use.

steps.title: State recommendations and follow-up
steps.detail: Turn what you observed into clear, actionable recommendations for the client: areas that need periodic deep treatment scheduled into a holiday window, matting or entry improvements to reduce tracked-in soil, a slip-resistance re-test interval, or a product change for routine cleaning. Where re-entry, dry or cure timing matters, point the client to the product SDS/TDS rather than quoting a fixed time, and coordinate the actual re-entry with the site contact.

steps.title: Add the limitations note, then review, sign and issue
steps.detail: Close with a short limitations statement: this is a professional service report supporting the client's decisions; it does not replace a product's SDS or TDS, the relevant Australian Standards, or a site's own rules, and this training and service are not IICRC-accredited and award no Continuing Education Credits (CEC) — IICRC S100 is referenced only as a recognised standard of care. Proofread for Australian spelling and internal consistency, sign and date the report, issue it to the client contact, and retain a copy with your job records.

safety: Anchor every control in the product SDS and your WHS duties; read the SDS before the job, not during an incident.
Record the SWMS or JSEA, PPE, ventilation and spill controls actually used — the report is evidence the job was done safely, not a formality.
Confirm all portable electrical equipment was within its AS/NZS 3760 test-and-tag interval and run on RCD (safety-switch) protected 230 V / 10 A GPO supply, and note it in the report.
Never state a fixed dry, cure or re-entry time in the report; those are product-specific and belong to the SDS/TDS.
Poisons Information is 13 11 26 — record it in first-aid sections where the SDS calls for it, and treat caustic-stripper and high-pressure-water tasks as genuine injury hazards.
childSafe: Record the correct state or territory Working With Children check for each worker on site, plus any site-specific child-safe induction.
Document that lower-tox products suited to occupied, child-occupied spaces were selected and diluted per label.
Record how wet, freshly stripped or freshly sealed floors were physically isolated and signed and kept away from children — a sign alone is not enough where children may not read or heed it.
Show how the work was scheduled around occupancy or into a term break / holiday window.
Frame child-safety measures as supporting National Quality Standard Quality Area 2 (children's health and safety); you support the centre's obligation, you do not certify it.
standards: Model WHS Regulations (Safe Work Australia) — Reg 346 hazardous chemicals register; current SDS (reviewed within 5 years) kept accessible; correct labelling including decanted containers. Victoria operates under its own OHS Act and Regulations.
GHS / Safe Work Australia — hazard classification and SDS structure referenced in the products-used section.
AS 4663 — slip resistance of existing (in-service) pedestrian surfaces; the basis for verifying a sealed floor against a target slip class.
AS 4586 — slip resistance classification of new pedestrian surfaces (pre-installation), noted to distinguish it from AS 4663.
SA HB 198 and the National Construction Code (NCC) — the source for recommended minimum slip classifications by location type (do not quote a rating value from memory).
AS/NZS 3760 — in-service safety inspection and testing (test-and-tag) of the portable electrical equipment recorded in the report.
ANSI/IICRC S100 — recognised standard of care for carpet cleaning (guidance, not law); this course/service is NOT IICRC-accredited and awards no CECs.
National Quality Framework / National Quality Standard Quality Area 2 (ACECQA) — the childcare children's-health-and-safety intent your measures support.
documentation: Report header: contractor legal name and ABN, site, client and site contact, contract/PO reference, attendance date(s), and service areas covered.
Pre-inspection findings per surface, including pre-existing damage and any target slip class.
Dated before-and-after photographs tied to each room or zone.
Scope of works and method chosen per surface, with product specifics deferred to SDS/TDS.
Products-used list cross-referenced to the current SDS register (each SDS within 5 years).
WHS record: SWMS/JSEA, PPE, ventilation, spill control, isolation and signage, and AS/NZS 3760 equipment status.
Child-safety record: WWC checks, site induction, low-tox selection, scheduling, isolation of wet/treated areas.
Run-off / trade-waste record for any pressure washing (containment, recovery, water-authority approval).
Slip-resistance verification (AS 4663) or arranged-testing note for any sealed floor.
Recommendations, follow-up and next periodic service window (verified against the contract spec).
Limitations statement, signature and date; retain a copy with job records.
contentBodyHtml:
Service Procedure — Reporting

A client-ready report is what turns a finished job into evidence a school or childcare centre can file, audit and act on. This procedure builds a single report that documents your pre-inspection findings, the agreed scope, the condition of each surface, the products and controls used, and the completion status across all four service areas — carpet, tile and grout, vinyl strip and seal, and pressure washing. It is not a marketing document; it is the paperwork layer that separates a casual job from a contract a client keeps renewing.

  1. Open with the job identifiers. Record the contractor's legal name and ABN, the site name and address, the client and site contact, the contract or purchase-order reference, and the date(s) of attendance. State which service areas the visit covered, because a strip-and-seal report carries very different risk and documentation from a routine carpet clean.
  2. Record the pre-inspection findings. Write up the fibre and construction, soil load and any pre-existing damage for carpet; the tile type, grout condition and any target slip class for tile and grout; and the existing finish and substrate condition for vinyl. Noting pre-existing damage up front protects both the client and you.
  3. Capture dated before-condition evidence. Photograph each area before work begins, with enough context that every image is identifiable later, and keep the images dated and tied to the room or zone.
  4. State the agreed scope of works per surface. Set out the method chosen and why — hot water extraction versus encapsulation for carpet; pre-wet, apply, dwell, agitate, rinse and recover for tile and grout; strip, rinse and neutralise, dry, then multiple thin sealer and finish coats for vinyl. Make clear that dilution ratios, coat counts and any drying or re-entry steps come from each product's SDS and TDS, not from a figure in the report.
  5. List the products used and reference the SDS register. Record every chemical by product name and supplier code, and confirm a current SDS (no more than five years old) is held for each in an accessible register. Where a product lacked a current SDS, record that you obtained it from the supplier before use.
  6. Document the WHS controls you applied. Record the SWMS or JSEA, the PPE worn per each SDS, the ventilation, spill controls, and how wet or treated floors were isolated and signed — and that all portable equipment was within its AS/NZS 3760 test-and-tag interval on RCD-protected supply.
  7. Record the child-safety measures. Record the workers' Working With Children checks and any site induction, the lower-tox product selection, the physical isolation and signage of wet or treated areas, and how the work was scheduled around occupancy or into a term break, framed as supporting NQS Quality Area 2.
  8. Record run-off control for any pressure washing. Document the containment, the recovery method, and the trade-waste approval from the water authority that allowed discharge to sewer, keeping wash water out of stormwater.
  9. Record slip-resistance verification where a floor was sealed. Note the target slip class (with reference to SA HB 198 and the NCC) and the AS 4663 result or arranged testing, and state clearly that AS 4663 measures slip resistance only and does not set re-entry times.
  10. Capture after-condition evidence and completion status. Photograph the finished areas from comparable positions and record each surface as complete, part-complete or deferred, flagging any floor still curing at the time of writing.
  11. State recommendations and follow-up. Turn observations into actionable recommendations — periodic deep treatments, matting improvements, a re-test interval — and point re-entry timing to the product SDS/TDS rather than quoting a fixed time.
  12. Add the limitations note, then review, sign and issue. Close with a short limitations statement, proofread for Australian spelling and consistency, sign and date, issue to the client, and retain a copy.

Safety

  • Anchor every control in the product SDS and your WHS duties; read the SDS before the job, not during an incident.
  • Record the SWMS or JSEA, PPE, ventilation and spill controls actually used — the report is evidence, not a formality.
  • Confirm and note that portable electrical equipment was within its AS/NZS 3760 test-and-tag interval and run on RCD-protected 230 V / 10 A supply.
  • Never state a fixed dry, cure or re-entry time; those belong to the SDS/TDS. Poisons Information is 13 11 26.

Child-safe

  • Record each worker's state or territory Working With Children check plus any site-specific child-safe induction.
  • Document lower-tox product selection and label-rate dilution for occupied, child-occupied spaces.
  • Record how wet, freshly stripped or freshly sealed floors were physically isolated and signed and kept away from children — a sign alone is not enough.
  • Show the work was scheduled around occupancy or into a term break / holiday window, supporting NQS Quality Area 2.

Standards

  • Model WHS Regulations — Reg 346 register, current SDS (within 5 years) kept accessible, correct labelling including decanted containers; Victoria under its own OHS Act.
  • AS 4663 (existing/in-service surfaces) for verifying a sealed floor; AS 4586 (new surfaces) noted for contrast; SA HB 198 and the NCC for recommended slip classes.
  • AS/NZS 3760 for the test-and-tag status of portable equipment; GHS / Safe Work Australia for SDS structure.
  • ANSI/IICRC S100 as a recognised standard of care only — this service is NOT IICRC-accredited and awards no CECs; NQF / NQS Quality Area 2 (ACECQA) for childcare health and safety.

Document this

  • Report header (contractor, site, client, contract/PO reference, dates, service areas).
  • Pre-inspection findings, dated before-and-after photos, and method chosen per surface with specifics deferred to SDS/TDS.
  • Products-used list cross-referenced to the current SDS register.
  • WHS record (SWMS/JSEA, PPE, ventilation, isolation, AS/NZS 3760 equipment status) and child-safety record (WWC, induction, low-tox, scheduling).
  • Run-off / trade-waste record for pressure washing and AS 4663 slip-resistance verification for any sealed floor.
  • Recommendations, next periodic window (verified against the contract spec), limitations statement, signature and retained copy.
